channel.title: Winston Lin
channel.description: Case studies, writing, and practical tools from work across AI workflows, serverless, developer platforms, and business systems.

item.title: Incident Timelines Need A Stable Shape
item.description: Incident reviews get more useful when the timeline is consistent enough to reduce debates about sequence, handoffs, and impact changes.

item.title: AI Work Needs Real Tools
item.description: Months of hands-on use reinforced that useful AI work is not clever prompting. It is tools, files, review loops, and recovery paths around the model.

item.title: Examples Are the Interface for Small Tools
item.description: For small public tools, a good example usually teaches faster than architecture talk because it shortens the gap between curiosity and proof.

item.title: Dependencies Need Owners Before They Need Slides
item.description: Planning artifacts become more useful when they expose overdue, unowned, and blocked work instead of just presenting milestones cleanly.

item.title: Public Repos Need a Short Path to First Useful Success
item.description: Public technical repos land better when they make the first useful success obvious with concrete examples, usable output, and honest limits.

item.title: Fun Projects Are How I Test Workflow Ideas
item.description: Small side projects are where I test workflow opinions, interface choices, and product taste without pretending every idea needs to become a company.

item.title: One-Page Tools Beat Premature Decks
item.description: When thinking is still rough, a one-page artifact usually beats a deck because it forces structure without dragging you into presentation theater too early.

item.title: Public Surfaces Should Route, Not Recite
item.description: A personal site, GitHub profile, and short notes should route people to the right depth instead of each trying to retell the whole story.

item.title: How I Use AI as a PM With a Real Workspace
item.description: The useful version of AI for product work is a real workspace that keeps notes, data, drafts, and assets in the same loop.

item.title: Growth Breaks In The Operating Model
item.description: A lot of growth problems are really handoff, pricing, and service-definition problems wearing a growth label.

item.title: AI Support Is A Workflow Design Problem
item.description: The hardest part of AI support is usually not the model. It is deciding what the system can do, when it should stop, and how it fits the real workflow.

item.title: Trustworthy AI Products Need Recovery Paths
item.description: Trust comes less from the best answer than from what the system does when it is unsure.

item.title: Developer Platforms Mostly Fail On Friction
item.description: Most platform products lose in the first hour, not in the roadmap review.

item.title: Feature Registries Are About Operational Clarity
item.description: A registry matters when it can answer what broke, who owns it, and what changed while something is already going wrong.

item.title: Migration Readiness Is A Product Problem
item.description: Migration pain is often designed into packaging, docs, and defaults long before a customer ever starts.

item.title: What Building Internal Platforms Taught Me
item.description: Internal platforms teach product judgment quickly because the users are technical, close to the work, and too busy to tolerate fluff.

item.title: Why I Still Like Small Python Tools
item.description: Small scripts still earn their keep when they remove one repeated annoyance from the week.
